speaker: interviewer
text: February 5, 1996; survivor, Zvi Michaeli, M-I-C-H-A-E-L-I; interviewer, Irene Squire; Hallendale, Florida; language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. Begin by telling us your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zvi Michaeli.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was-- Isaac Zvi-- Isaac [INAUDIBLE] Michalowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever use any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michalowski or Micaeli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in Ejszyszki.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine age right now is 71.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your early life in Ejszyszki before the onslaught of Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I left-- I left to come back to the house, [BACKROUND NOISE] since I remember Ejszyszki. That's about 55 years ago, or 70 years ago, whichever you say. So [INAUDIBLE] now start to be-- in order to portrayed Ejszyszki, at it was, I have to entirely ignore the place where I am interview, ignore America, ignore wherever I was, and come back to Ejszyski. Then I need to be-- to turn over myself to a kid and to describe you the Ejszyszki like it was-- like hundreds and thousands of shtetl in Lithuania and on an Eastern Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was Ejszyszki also, which a common shtetl, Jewish shtetl, with a yeshiva, with a [NON-ENGLISH] shul with all kind of Jewish activities, like [NON-ENGLISH], with a [NON-ENGLISH], and three shuls, and all kind of activities, Jewish activities, with organizations, like Betar, and Hashomer Hatzair, and Mizrahi, and a nice, beautiful, Jewish youth-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And the life was going on normally.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was your family life like? How many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six children-- and, of course, a big family. Besides our family, mom-- my mother's two sisters, my father has a brother with a family, with five children; mine uncle from the mother's side with three children; and the other aunt with two children. And the life was going on so normal, everybody do his duty. I was, that time, a young man going in the yeshiva, learning mainly Jewish studies, Jewish history, mainly about religious things. My father was a teacher-- Hebrew and Yiddish, and also in Talmud. And in this kind atmosphere, I grew up. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Till 1939 was going on, like it was going on years before, for hundreds years or more-- Ejszyszki was in existence, like according to the history of Ejszyszki, for thousands years-- Jewish life in Ejszyszki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard anything about Kristallnacht in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I was, that time, a young-- a young boy, 13-years-old. But we feel it also not only hearing it about in the radio, in the-- in the press, but also German refugees start to come in-- in-- when they were telling what's happening in Germany. But it was-- is so close, so just a couple hundred miles away. But it was so far from us, that it's not going to happen here. And the life was going on very normal. And then when the war break out with Poland, our region was taken over from the Russian. And they-- they gave it over to the Lithuanian, because Ejszyszki was before the first world war. It belonged to Lithuanian today. So they give it over to the Lithuanian. But life wasn't change. In-- and Jewish life, particularly, wasn't changed any difference from like it was when-- under the Polish regime. And we didn't feel, actually, any difference. And eight months later, the Russian took it over-- Lithuania. They occupied Lithuanian again. And we were a part, like a Lithuanian Republic of Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '40-- 1940, shortly after the second world war start. And we were all living, I would say, in peaceful manners, till the second world war-- till June 22nd, 1941, when Hitler decide to occupied Russia, in particular, not even declare war on Russia. And we were occupied the second day. The 23rd was the first Russian soldiers came in into our city. And the army flew so fast eastward-- like nobody stops him. And-- and after the [SIGHS] Russian occupation, we start to feel what that mean-- German occupation for Jews. One day, some difference-- I'd like to say-- orders for the Jewish people, like a Jew shouldn't go on the sidewalk-- should walk on the middle of the streets; announcing on the walls with big, official announcement, Jews should carry over to the authorities furs, silver, gold, livestocks, to the local authorities. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They used to-- they form a judenrat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we used to go. They used to-- the judenrat used to appoint work force for the German authority and for the Lithuanian authority, whenever there were request. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [SIGHS] little by little, we adjust ourself under these new circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I used-- I was, at that time, about 15, 16 years old already-- 16 years old already. I used to go to work, even I wasn't-- I don't need to go to work, 'cause of mine age. But I used to go to work instead of my father, because I didn't want to exploit my father to-- to the Germans. They used to-- like to say in English-- they used to laugh on-- from the Jewish who wears a beard. Who wears payot. My father was a clean, with a little beard. They used to make jokes. And they used to-- yeah. I mean, it was impossible. It wasn't-- so I was going for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was going to fixing the roads-- 10, 15, kilometers from every day, back and forth. But that wasn't the worst one. We got to adjust ourself. They marched-- the worst thing the-- what they hurt my feeling, that we not allowed to walk on the sidewalks. That was so embarrassing-- humiliating us-- that all the Christian kids, they used to laugh. They're going on a sidewalk with their head up. And we are not allowed to go on the sidewalk. In normal-- in normal times, I would never-- I would never talk to these kind of guys. And they are going with their head up. Now we are like second, third class, according to them. But that's also was-- you know, you can live with that, when you get used to. Everybody used to worry somehow to get some food from the neighbor-- from the farms around. And we used to manage. An particularly incident, I would like to point out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] The German came in from the-- first week, they came in with a list-- particular names. And they took out the names. They took out the people who were, according them, communists, or participating with the Communist Party. They used to be employed for the Communist Party in Ejszyszki. I don't know how they got the names. My sister, Judy, the oldest one, wasn't living in Ejszyszki in this time of-- of the occupation from Russia. After they took over Lithuania, she went to a neighborhood city called Lida. But they got her on the list also. One day, a German-- tall guy-- I don't know his rank. I-- I don't know the German ranks, the military ranks. German guy-- and he asked for Judith, for my sister. I was only-- because of that, nobody of the sisters was home. They were in hiding. Not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not-- not really in hiding. They were on different houses, like by their boyfriends, or by aunts, by stranger people, [BACKROUND NOISE] just not to be home. When they come to look for Judy, they shouldn't be home. They can pick up them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was home with my mother. And the German asked, where is Judy? And my mother say, she doesn't know. Judy is-- 19-- it's still 1939-- not in Ejszyszki. And he gave her a kick in the stomach. But she fall down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I start to cry. And I don't know what to do. So I-- I don't know how it came to me the courage. I pick-- I took him for the arm. I-- I should-- nobody should touch a German soldier, an officer. And I say, come. I'll show you where Judy is-- just to get him away from my mother. And she was laying on the floor, already in pain. He came out after me. He's coming after me. From the back door, I took him out. And in between our two houses was a separation. And we used to-- children and play, and hidings, when we were children out there and hidings, on all kind of games playing there. And this was closed with a plywood-- you know, not to get in. But it was like when you're pulling the plywood, it was like a spring when you left it. At the top was nailed. And it was loose on the bottom. So I said, there is Judy. And I opened up the-- with force, I opened up the bottom of the plywood. He got in. I left it, and I closed it. And I start to run. When he got stuck in there-- it tooks him a while-- like two minutes or three minutes-- till he got out. And I ran away about five kilometers, to a-- to a Polish peasant farmer, what I knew him. They used to come to us. He came back home. And he beat up my mother so badly that they took her into the hospital. Well, now got-- they start to be after me. But I was on the farm. And that was a German, a passing by German. It's like a [NON-ENGLISH]. They're not stationing in the-- there that was a SS, an army SS. After two days, they left. The unit left Ejszyszki. And we almost forgot the incident. I wasn't in-- at home. For three days, I was in the peasant house in the farm, hiding in the barn. And that's the way we live till Rosh Hashanah, 1941-- all kind of humiliations, embarrassing the Jewish people. And 1941, Rosh Hashanah came. A order came that all-- all the Jewish people had to concentrate in the three synagogues from our shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the order come?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Lithuanian police came from street to street, from house to house. Everybody should concentrate them-- all the Jewish in the three synagogues. And it was Rosh Hashanah. You know, everybody got a little something prepare, according his-- possibilities. But everybody has something on Rosh Hashanah-- challahs, soup, [BACKROUND NOISE] and chicken, whatever they're preparing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mine three sisters, like I tell you before, wasn't at home at that time. They were over at their boyfriends or with their girlfriends. I came. I was-- is at home with my younger brother, my mother, and my father, and also a sister of my grandmother. She was living with us. When I came in our home, I find only my mother and my-- and my brother. My father wasn't already-- he was already in the shul, because I wasn't home. When he told my mother he's going to be in the-- in the shul-- in the picture, I'll show you-- I took mine brother. And my mother suggest not to go to the shul, but to run away to the farm where I was in hiding before. I took my mother-- she gives me a challah, I remember, on the way. I took my brother, and we run-- start to run to Horbaczewski, to the farm where I was in hiding before. It was about five kilometer from-- from the town. But as soon as we came-- we're getting out of borders of the town-- the Lithuanian was blocking. And they turned us-- us back to the synagogue. I came into the synagogue. I find my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at the-- in one hour, the shul was packed. And maybe I would say by 900 people, by 1,000 people, with the children, with-- with everything, with all the-- everybody was-- it was-- could carry on from their house into the shul. I find there also my mother. She's still in there, with her son, and her husband, and the other mother's sister, with her husband. And-- and we're sitting down. And it's panic-- the yelling and the screaming, and-- and-- and-- and-- and the crying from the children, from all over-- was so terrible. And everybody were trying to find a place even to sit down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were sitting close to each other-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] sitting like this for two hours. You know, people need to do their need for what they need to do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And people start to yell and to step on each other. And they decide, on the front of the shul, in the foyer, there should be the place to go to make-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] women and children, and the men, and women, all together. [SIGHS] I need-- one time, I need to go. And I went over people, over children, over clothes, over feets, over heads. And the yelling, and the screaming, that was a terrible scenery. When I came in the back-- actually, it was the entrance from the shul, but the doors was closed already. It's already was-- you're stepping in there. It was already like to the ankles full with, really, shit. But you have no choice. You got to do it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came back. It was a job to go back to the place where I start to. My father was sitting all the way on the-- his side of the shul. And the Mizrach want. He was from the first one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rabbi, Szulim, start to pray. It was Friday night, Rosh Hashanah night. He start to pray. And the yelling with the praying, it was so touch that we-- or everybody start to cry while we were praying. And suddenly, while I was sitting with my father and my mother, who appears near us? Judith. My-- Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judith, my sister. When my father saw Judith, I saw him getting older and gray in minutes. The pain that Judith is here-- he was sure she'll manage to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever will happen to us? Whatever will happen will happen. But she's in the first fire. She's on the list. They'll get her out and shoot her right away. So he asked me, [NON-ENGLISH]. Try to get her out from the shul. He was asking me with such a-- worries, that I couldn't refuse him. I couldn't say no. But from the other hand, I knew it's just impossible. We'll never make it to get out. But I say, OK. Let's try.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was going to make in the front, a little window was open from there. The Lithuanian allowed, because of the-- because of the air there. And I took Judy. We went through over the whole things, and we got out the windows. Nobody stopped. When I took her to Radun, at night-- it was already night. I know the back ways to go. I took her to Radun, and I left her in a-- a-- second cousins of my father, in their home. They call her [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]? Who was doing oh, things from wood. He was doing all kind of work for-- for the peasants, for the-- for the farmers, when they were weaving the-- the knits.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause here to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
